sentences.text: My name is Max Sitzer, M-A-X, S-I-T-Z-E-R.
I was born May the 25th, 1925, in a little village called Stremilcze, S-T-R-E-M-I-L-C-Z-E, and that's in the eastern-- in the-- in the Ukraine.
OK. it's a village.
It-- it's got 180 families, mostly Ukrainian.
Nine-- among the 180 were 9 Jewish families in that little town-- in that little village.
And we had a shul.
My father was one of the organizers.
He build the shul.
And every Saturday, we used have a-- a service, and holidays, too.
Every day, the shul was-- by the ways, the shul was closed during the week.
Now we are-- my father was actually a farm.
We have-- we had a farm of 20 acres.
My mother-- we had a general store, which my mother looked after it.
And my father was heavy involved in real estate-- buying and selling real estate.
I was-- before the war, I was 14.
I was going to a Hebrew school called Tarbut.
And that was 5 kilometers from my town.
And on the weekend, I used to come home.
In the summer, I used to help out on the farm.
And that's how I was till 1939, till the war broke out.
OK, I had four-- I had four sisters.
Actually, till '39, I had only three sisters, because the fourth was born in 1941 during the-- already during the Holocaust when the Germans were there.
So I had two older sisters and one younger sister at that time.
And then I had another sister born in '41.
My oldest sister was born 1920.
My second oldest was born in '22, I was born in '25, and my younger sister was born in '29.
And the fourth sister was born 1941.
Now, my oldest sister was also going to the school in-- in a school, to the-- called Berestechko.
It was 5 kilometers from Stremilcze.
And then my sister, when she turned 16, my father send her to Israel to study agriculture, near Jerusalem.
And then she came back in 1938.
My young-- my second oldest sister was also going to Berestechko.
And my third older sister was going also with me in-- in school in Berestechko.
Because in Stremilcze was only four grade-- a grade four.
That was the requirement the Polish government requires.
You have to finish grade four.
After grade four, you on your own.
So whoever wanted to go farther to school had to go someplace else, because that's-- that was it.
My sister's-- the oldest-- name was Walerka, W-A-L-E-R-K-A.
My second oldest sister was Helena, H-E-L-E-N-A.
My-- my name, I already said it.
And my youngest sister was Edzia, E-D-Z-I-A.
And my second youngest sister was Bronia, B-R-O-N-I-A.
My father's name was David, and my mother's name was Lea, L-E-A.
OK.
We had the-- like, we were very modern at home.
Our house wasn't actually kosher, but we were traditional Jews.
We believed in that-- in Judaism, and we were pro-Zionist.
And I, even as a child, was already active in Hashomer Hatzair, OK?
And my oldest sister was-- my-- the one that went to Israel-- she was-- she belonged to a-- to a organization-- the [NON-ENGLISH].
That was Jabotinsky's party.
There, then this party became later Likud.
And my second older sister, she didn't take any part, and went to school.
That's in the-- and the rest-- the Shabbat.
The-- every Friday we used to have-- Friday night, we used to go to shul, come home, have Shabbat dinner.
In Shabbat we used to take [NON-ENGLISH].
I mean, from shul, it was always some poor people which were looking for a meal.
So every Friday night, that was a custom.
Everybody else-- some-- everybody else took-- and it-- it's an Hebrew called [HEBREW], or a poor man.
So we used to come in and feed him with the dinner.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father was-- in the summer-- he worked in the fields.
But we also had hired help.
And we had a good general store, like I said before.
And we were well off.
We were--
Oh, it was from 1919 till-- till-- till the-- the Holocaust-- from 1919.
My-- my-- my-- my father was born in 1919.
My father was married in 1919, and he came to Stremilcze.
And then where he set up-- and he made-- and he start doing business there.
Yeah, my grandmother and my grandfather was there, yeah.
To the non-Jews?
Fair, fair, because you see, we had-- there was another Jewish family.
They had a store also.
And the rest were more or less-- it was one guy-- he was-- it was one-- mostly were farmers.
You know, and some were tradesmen only.
You know, they were buying and selling stuff.
But that-- that's all.
And-- and the rest of the-- the community-- all were farmers-- all farmers.
I couldn't say-- I wouldn't say that there was, actually.
Later, in 1936, '37, where after Hitler came to power in Germany, that's when we felt the anti-Semitism start blowing up-- blowing out.
You see, in mainly in the big cities like Lemberg, that was there, you know.
It was a different-- that's where it were, the anti-Semitism was.
But in-- in the little-- in my little town, I didn't really feel it.
In 1936, in that particular town of Berestechko, that was a-- that was already maybe-- over there it could be about 500 or 600 Jewish families.
Yeah, so there, in 1936-- I remember when I went to school in '37, '38-- was big transprints-- don't buy at the Jews; Jews to Palestine-- such a thing, you know.
But that was in '36, and '37, and '38, up to till the war.
That was-- that was-- I remember, I used to go there.
It used to be big.
Don't buy at Jews.
Jews, go to Palestine, and, you know.
The Nazis-- from the papers.
I actually knew in '34 or '35.
We didn't know, you know, what they were actually-- but we knew this-- the Nazis, because in-- in Lemberg, we had a university.
And two Jews were killed in the-- on the campuses.
So, you know, and we knew.
One of the Jews we knew, because he had a-- he was a big landowner near us.
And his son-- that was his son who got killed on the campus.
So at that time, I remember, the paper wrote that's all blowing over from Germany, from Hitler.
The papers wrote that.
The Polish and the Jewish press wrote that.
So I remember reading in the paper.
It was-- Do you remember his name?
It's-- it's Zellemier Yeah, I remember well his name.
They were a family-- actually, they were very rich.
And one of the sons went to the Spanish-- you know when the Spanish war was on?
So he went to fight on the-- on the left.
And his father was a-- a millionaire.
You know, when-- when the Russians came in in 1939, they name a street by the name Zellemier-- is-- you want me to spell it?
I know.
Sure.
Z-E-L-L-E-M-I-E-R, exactly, Zellemier.
His first name, I don't know.
So that's-- that's the incident I remember, you know?
The other guy, I didn't know.
Not really, not-- I mean, it was they used to call you a lousy Jew, or this.
But it was not really, you know, begins.
No.
I can't say it.
And you know, the-- the Ukrainian were doing business, because they used to come in and buy.
Money was very, you know, scarce.
What they-- for instance, they bring in grain and trade, OK?
Or they take on credit, and after he sells a pig, or two pigs, or he sells a cow or this, he comes in.
He pays so much, and buys again.
And that was the kind of a business in the store.
And my father, well, we-- we got the 20 acres.
Every time he used to do a deal, a real estate deal, so he used to leave himself a parcel, you know.
And that's how we accumulate 20 acres of land.
In Poland, it was a big farm at 20 acres.
Here it's not, but in Poland it was a big-- it was a very good size of farm-- 20 acres.
We had two barns, five cows, two horses, chickens, and all small stuff, you know?
Did you have a bigger family outside of your immediate family-- cousins, aunts, uncles?
Yeah, but not-- yeah, we had there.
We had there in that village.
We had five cousins.
That was my mother's sister's kids-- five boys.
Because they were living in the same village, and they were all-- they didn't have-- the mother-- the mother got killed in 1919 when she was holding the youngest kid.
It was a-- a stray bullet went through and killed the mother, and the little baby was left.
So they were all left, you know, without a mother.
So they were-- and they were poor, you know so they used come in and have dinner with us, and so on, and so on.
So that's-- that was my five cousins.
Before the war?
No, just helping out in the summer on my vacation-- take the workers to the field, bring 'em back-- you know, that kind.
I didn't work.
In 9-- before the war, when we expect to-- we didn't actually expect to get bad.
The Russian-- you know, in 1939, the war started, our part was occupied by the Russians.
So when they came in, we were two years under the Russians-- from-- actually, from September 1939 to June '41-- not quite two years.
And during that time, I was in school, and my father was doing some little business-- not too much.
But they-- we were expecting that they going to take us to Siberia, because we were wealthy, you know.
But it didn't happen, unfortunately.
You know, later it shows there's-- whoever they took was alive, and we are-- and our family perished in the Holocaust.
The first day of the war, the-- with the war broke out, and I mean, not-- when the Nazis came in, or the '39?
The Russians came-- came through.
The troops came through.
There was no incident, you know.
They stopped some headquarters in our house, because we had a big house.
So they took a room.
They ask their permission-- our permission.
We told them it's OK.
And then actually, during the Russian occupation, we lived-- we lived more or less a normal life.
We didn't like it, because they were confiscating-- they were talking about making collective farms, taking away our land.
We didn't have any business already, but in the meantime, we were working on our land and-- and the whole problem start when the-- when the Germans came in in 1941.
And the 22nd of June, the war broke out.
And the 23rd, 10 o'clock in the morning, the Germans were already in our village, because it was 50 kilometers from the border.
And in the first day, we-- we had, right away, bitter experience.
My sister walked out on the street, and-- and a German car pulled in, pulled down the window when [? she ?]-- and they called her in and spit in her face.
That's an incident I remember, in '41.
She came in.
She was crying.
So I says, what happened?
So she says to me, you know what happened?
That's what happened.
Told me the story, you know, and it really bothered me.
I says, I know mine chances are 99% I'll probably not live through.
If I ever live through, I'll-- I'll-- I'll go.
It doesn't matter if I get killed or not, I'll go.
And I lived through, and I went to the Russian army, you know?
And I did what I had to do, you know?
[SIGHS] But then, during the Holocaust-- during-- you see, the first-- we-- from June '41 to September '42 we were still-- they kicked us out from our house.
They-- we wanted to-- they gave us a little house.
And the big house, our house was-- 1/2 of the house was the-- it was like the-- they had the-- what do you call that?
The-- it was the head of the village, you know, the office.
And the other 1/2 of the house was the police department.
And we lived in that old house, but our village is-- they established a border, because in 1914, our village was the border between Austria and Russia.
And the German kept the same exactly thing.
So they brought in, and they-- they established a border.
And they brought in a Grenzschitzkommando, like a border patrol, you know?
And that patrol, they set up in school.

And every Jewish household is supposed to send somebody to service them-- clean their boots, heat their, you know, they have-- we had to cut the-- cut wood, and so on, and clean the rooms, and so on, and so on, and so on.
So I was the-- I was only boy in my household.
So my father said, you go.

So every day I used to get up in the morning and-- and go there, and spend there maybe four, five hours there, you know, and do casual work, like cut the wood, and-- and clean the rooms, and-- and clean their boots, and all kind of-- that kind of work.
And they were, at that time, it was more or less, you know, you see, at-- at Berestechko, our-- our part called [NON-ENGLISH].
Other words, that was Polish occupied-- occupied land.
In Berestechko just 5 kilometers from there, was already Russian.
They figured that that's-- there's entirely different-- difference laws, entirely-- they-- they got their ghetto.
They build a ghetto right away.
And they took 'em in the ghetto.
And they-- they murdered the Jews a lot-- six months before they-- we ever had-- had the first actzia, you know?
I remember that commandant from our border patrol came over and he said to me, he says, [NON-ENGLISH].
And he showed me like that, you know?
That's-- they went.
And they went to help there to shoot the-- so it not only them, it's a few other Ukrainian, which we took care later when the Russian came back, you know?
So we knew about that about six months earlier than-- than we-- you know?
And then I and-- and then in September, just before Rosh Hashanah, they-- we had the first actzia.
My father, my two sisters weren't home.
Me and my two sisters, my younger two sisters, were home-- and my mother.
So they came in.
And they took us just as we stood-- nothing.
You don't take nothing with-- put us on a-- by a wagon-- horse and buggy, and they took us to a town, [NON-ENGLISH].
From [NON-ENGLISH], they took us to a town-- we had to walk to Kamionka Strumilowa.
In Kamionka, they loaded us on a freight train.
And then we knew already that's where we going.
Because the rumors are that they're taking us to Belzec.
And the freight train was blocked up with boards, but somehow me and another guy-- you know, the-- the guards were in the last-- in the last wagon.
Me and another guy, and another couple guys managed to-- to get loose those boards.
And I remember an old man told me he didn't want to jump.
My mother encouraged me to jump.
The old guy told me.
He says, when you jump, don't jump backwards, just jump with the-- with the train.
Otherwise, you'll get killed.
And I-- I jumped.
I jumped.
I got hurt.
I know it was night when I jumped.
Then when I woke up, it was daytime.
I didn't know where I was.
So I came into the near-- near village.
And I asked.
And they told me.
So I start walking back.
At that time, the Germans had called actzias.
They need a certain quota of-- of Jews.
For instance, if you had-- if they need 1,000 and you're the 1,001, they didn't take you.
So you go the next time or whatever.
So, this-- you know, when I jumped, I start walking.
I came in.
I had a girlfriend in a little village.
So I was walking back there.
You know, that was already-- I was 17 years of age by then in '42.
So I came into the village, to that village.
It took me maybe a whole day to walk.
And I found the girlfriend.
I found-- that time I met my father, because my father didn't go.
He was-- it just happened.
I wasn't supposed to be at home.
My father worked in the field for the Germans.
The Germans-- all the Jew-- the-- the big landowners, the Jewish, they took away the land-- made the Jews work the land.
And they-- they, you know, they didn't pay them, actually-- nothing.
So my father was working, and my two sisters were there.
And when they heard that an actzia going on-- so they already, you know-- we prepared.
Because we knew what an actzia was by that time.
Because in different cities, it was before us.
So when I came back, I met my father and my two sisters.
And the two sisters and my mother went to Belzec.
So at that time, my father says, you know what?
We got to send the sisters on Ukrainian papers to Germany.
Went to a-- to a village called Baryliv.
But as foreign-- as two-- birth certificates-- paid him, because he had some of my-- of my mother's jewelry-- paid the priest.
And my father send 'em to Germany.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] One-- no, it's-- it's OK.
My wife will answer.
My-- my father-- so he send them to Germany.
One arrived in Germany.
We got a letter.
The other one, we never knew what happened.
The-- the other-- the oldest sister arrived in Germany, worked in Germany till 1944.
We found out after the war.
And then in '44, she commits suicide.
For what reason-- unknown to me.
But--
Pardon?
Sister, yeah, the older sister.
The second oldest, we don't know what happened to her.
She didn't arrive in Germany.
We don't know.
Maybe somebody recognized her, you know?
We don't know.
We still don't know.
So-- but after the war, we knew the name-- under what name-- she was under the name [NON-ENGLISH], under Ukrainian name.
So we went to Germany, at that address.
We went, we found the-- the guy who was in charge of that factory.
And he told us.
He said, she committed suicide, and she's buried there in the cemetery.
We came in.
And a cross there, because she was, you know, under Ukrainian name.
So they-- so we-- me and my father, you know, took the remains.
And we shipped her to Israel and buried in Jerusalem.
And that's where my father is buried, near-- you know, when he bought a plot in Jerusalem, [NON-ENGLISH], he made sure he bought an extra plot.
And he built everything just to-- when he passed away he moved the stone.
So that's was short.
But during the Holocaust-- OK.
Yeah.
After-- after I came back to my-- with my girl-- to my girlfriend, that-- so it's a lot of guys came back, which they lost their mother, their father, their sisters, their husband.
So it was people which 1/2 of the families were already gone.
But that was a rich farmer, the-- my girlfriend's father, and he didn't care.
So one day, it was in October, 1942-- I don't know the date.
The Germans came in-- two-- two Ukrainian policeman and one German.
They-- they-- it was about 16 of us.
You know, mostly it was that family, Kranc, which was the last name of my girlfriend's-- that was her last name.
Her father, and her-- one of the sisters, and her future husband, wasn't there.
Otherwise, the whole family was there.
The mother, she had a sister, a brother-in-law.
Anyway, to make the story-- they-- they got-- they found some booze.
They got drunk in that house.
And they took us out.
It was not too far.
It was a little forest.
We knew exactly where we're going.
So I was wearing two jackets, but I didn't have any shoes.
For some reason, I didn't have any shoes.
And-- and so I was going to run right there.
But a guy near me, Dowcze Apelfeld, he says, look Maxi.
If you going to run, run closer to the forest so you have a chance.
Otherwise, on the field they'll catch you for sure.
So I listened to him.
I came in.
But that was just like that, you know?
The-- the Ukrainian was on the-- on the ground.
The policemen, the German, was on the-- on the horse and buggy, you know, sitting there.
So I see in my girlfriend got cut down right in front of me.
Another sister-- but that was seconds.
I ran-- I ran in the forest and fell down.
So they thought they got me.
But then I start thinking, and I said, no.
The forest is a little forest that they'll-- so I start-- it was a potato field.
So I ran out there.
And when they saw me run and they start shooting-- but didn't get me.
So I laid down there till it was late at night.
At night I knew there's the father and the sister-- one of the sisters.
Yeah, whoever-- when I looked after when I start running, a little boy, Yoshi-- he was about 10 years old-- he ran too.
That was one of the family of my-- my girlfriend's brother.
They only had one boy, and six-- five or six girls.
But anyway, I ran.
And then from the potato field I went to a farmer.
OK.
